title: Migrate database credentials from ConfigMap to Secrets and eliminate environment variable exposure
description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap currently contains the PostgreSQL connection URL with the username in plaintext (postgresql://sigma1_user@...). Multiple service Deployments use envFrom with configMapRef, which injects all ConfigMap values as environment variables — including connection strings that may be logged by frameworks or exposed via /proc/environ if a container is compromised. This task remediates by: (1) removing all credential components from the ConfigMap, (2) creating a dedicated Kubernetes Secret for database connection parameters, (3) mounting connection strings as Secret volumes (projected volumes) rather than environment variables, and (4) updating all service Deployments to consume credentials from projected Secret volumes instead of envFrom configMapRef.
subtasks: Remove CNPG_SIGMA1_URL from sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap entirely — replace with non-sensitive keys only (VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL without auth, R2_ENDPOINT, R2_BUCKET, SIGNAL_CLI_URL)

Create a new Kubernetes Secret sigma1-db-connection containing the full PostgreSQL connection URL (with username) as a key, referencing the CNPG-generated sigma1-postgres-app Secret for the password component

Update all five service Deployments (equipment-catalog, rms, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine) to mount sigma1-db-connection and sigma1-postgres-app as projected Secret volumes at /etc/sigma1/db/ instead of using envFrom configMapRef for database credentials

Update each service's application initialization code to read the database connection string from the mounted file at /etc/sigma1/db/connection-url rather than from the DATABASE_URL environment variable

Remove the configMapRef entry for sigma1-infra-endpoints from envFrom in all Deployments — instead selectively inject only non-sensitive ConfigMap keys as individual env vars using valueFrom.configMapKeyRef

Verify no environment variable in any running pod contains database usernames or connection strings by running: kubectl exec <pod> -- env | grep -i 'postgres\|pgpass\|database_url' and confirming empty output
Run integration tests for all five services to confirm they successfully connect to PostgreSQL using the file-mounted credentials
acceptance_criteria: No ConfigMap in the sigma1 namespace contains any database username, password, or connection URL
All database credentials are stored exclusively in Kubernetes Secrets
No service pod has database connection strings exposed as environment variables (verified via kubectl exec -- env)
All five backend services successfully connect to PostgreSQL and pass their /health/ready check after the migration
Connection strings are mounted as read-only files via projected volumes at /etc/sigma1/db/
The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap contains only non-sensitive infrastructure endpoints (Valkey URL without auth, R2 endpoint, R2 bucket name, Signal-CLI URL)

title: Implement asymmetric JWT signing with JWKS endpoint and key rotation support
description: A single shared JWT_SECRET (symmetric HS256) is currently used across all services for both signing and verification. Compromise of any one service exposes the signing key for all services, and there is no key rotation mechanism. This task switches to asymmetric JWT signing (RS256 or ES256) with a centralized JWKS endpoint, implements support for multiple active verification keys to enable rotation, and documents the key rotation procedure.
subtasks: Generate an RSA-2048 or EC P-256 key pair for JWT signing. Store the private key in a new Kubernetes Secret sigma1-jwt-signing-key accessible only to the token-issuing service (Morgan or a dedicated auth component)

Create a JWKS endpoint (GET /.well-known/jwks.json) in the token-issuing service that exposes the public key(s) in JWK format with key IDs (kid). Support serving multiple public keys simultaneously for rotation overlap periods

Update the equipment-catalog JWT middleware to validate tokens using RS256/ES256 by fetching and caching the JWKS public keys from the issuing service. Implement JWKS cache with 5-minute TTL and automatic refresh on kid mismatch
Update the RMS service JWT validation to use the JWKS endpoint with the same caching strategy
Update the finance service JWT middleware to use asymmetric verification via JWKS
Update the customer-vetting service JWT middleware to use asymmetric verification via JWKS
Update the social-engine Elysia JWT plugin to validate against the JWKS endpoint using a compatible jose/jwks library

Remove the sigma1-jwt-secret Kubernetes Secret from all service Deployments that only need to verify tokens (all except the issuer). Only the token-issuing service should have access to the private signing key

Document the JWT key rotation procedure in ops/runbooks/jwt-key-rotation.md: generate new key pair → add new public key to JWKS → wait for cache expiry across services → sign new tokens with new key → remove old public key after grace period
Write integration tests verifying: (1) tokens signed with the current key validate successfully, (2) tokens signed with a rotated-out key are rejected, (3) JWKS endpoint returns valid JWK set
acceptance_criteria: All JWT tokens are signed using RS256 or ES256 (asymmetric) instead of HS256 (symmetric)
A JWKS endpoint exists and returns valid JWK JSON with at least one public key including a kid field
Only the token-issuing service has access to the private signing key Secret
All five backend services validate JWTs by fetching public keys from the JWKS endpoint
JWKS response supports multiple keys simultaneously for rotation overlap
Tokens signed with a revoked/rotated key are rejected with HTTP 401
ops/runbooks/jwt-key-rotation.md exists with step-by-step rotation procedure
The sigma1-jwt-secret shared Secret is removed from all non-issuer service Deployments

title: Strengthen GDPR internal endpoint authentication with cryptographic tokens
description: GDPR export and delete endpoints across all five services are protected only by a simple X-Internal-Request or X-Internal-Key header check with a static string. These endpoints perform destructive, irreversible operations (data deletion and anonymization). A compromised pod within the allowed network segment could spoof these headers. Additionally, there is no rate limiting on GDPR delete endpoints, allowing mass deletion from a compromised orchestrator. This task implements stronger authentication using a dedicated internal JWT signed with a separate key pair, adds rate limiting, and adds mandatory audit logging before execution.
subtasks: Generate a dedicated GDPR operations key pair (ES256) separate from the main JWT signing key. Store the private key in a Kubernetes Secret sigma1-gdpr-signing-key accessible only to the Morgan/GDPR orchestrator pod

Implement a GDPR token issuer in the Morgan/orchestrator service that mints short-lived JWTs (5-minute expiry) with claims: operation (export|delete), customer_id, issued_at, and a unique request_id for idempotency

Update the equipment-catalog /internal/gdpr/* endpoints to validate the GDPR-specific JWT (not the user JWT) using the GDPR public key. Reject requests with missing, expired, or invalid tokens with HTTP 401
Update the RMS /internal/gdpr/* endpoints with the same GDPR JWT validation
Update the finance /internal/gdpr/* endpoints with GDPR JWT validation
Update the customer-vetting /internal/gdpr/* endpoints with GDPR JWT validation
Update the social-engine /internal/gdpr/* endpoints with GDPR JWT validation

Implement rate limiting on all GDPR delete endpoints: maximum 10 delete operations per minute per service, using a Valkey sliding window counter with key gdpr_delete_rate:{service_name}. Return HTTP 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded

Add mandatory pre-execution audit logging: before any GDPR delete operation executes, INSERT a record into the audit.gdpr_operations table with: request_id, customer_id, operation_type, requesting_service, requesting_identity (from JWT), timestamp, status=initiated. Update status to completed or failed after execution

Write integration tests: (1) GDPR delete without valid GDPR JWT returns 401, (2) GDPR delete with expired JWT returns 401, (3) 11th delete request within 1 minute returns 429, (4) audit table contains record before and after deletion
acceptance_criteria: All GDPR internal endpoints require a valid, short-lived GDPR-specific JWT (not a user JWT or static header)
The GDPR signing key is separate from the main JWT signing key and accessible only to the orchestrator
GDPR JWTs expire within 5 minutes of issuance
Rate limiting enforces maximum 10 delete operations per minute per service endpoint
Every GDPR delete operation has a corresponding audit record in audit.gdpr_operations with status tracking
The static X-Internal-Key / X-Internal-Request header check is removed from all GDPR endpoints
Requests with missing, expired, or invalid GDPR tokens return HTTP 401

title: Harden Stripe webhook signature validation implementation
description: The Finance service Stripe webhook handler has an underspecified implementation for signature validation. Incorrect Stripe webhook signature validation is a common source of payment fraud. The raw body must be read before any parsing/deserialization, which Axum's default body extraction may interfere with. This task ensures the implementation is correct, timing-safe, and includes replay protection.
subtasks: Implement a dedicated Axum extractor (RawBody) that reads the request body as raw bytes before any JSON deserialization occurs. This extractor must be used before any Json<T> extractor in the webhook handler signature
Use the stripe-rust crate's Webhook::construct_event() function for signature validation rather than manual HMAC computation, as it handles timing-safe comparison and timestamp tolerance internally
Configure the Stripe webhook endpoint to reject events with timestamps older than 5 minutes (Stripe's default tolerance) to prevent replay attacks — verify construct_event enforces this
Add explicit error handling: return HTTP 400 with a generic error message (no internal details) for invalid signatures, and log the failure details at WARN level with the source IP

Write test cases for: (1) valid signature passes and event is processed, (2) tampered payload with original signature returns 400, (3) valid payload with wrong webhook secret returns 400, (4) replay attack with stale timestamp (>5 minutes old) returns 400, (5) verify the raw body extractor prevents Axum from consuming the body before signature validation
acceptance_criteria: Stripe webhook endpoint reads the raw request body as bytes before any JSON deserialization
Signature validation uses the stripe-rust Webhook::construct_event() function with timing-safe comparison
Events with timestamps older than 5 minutes are rejected (replay protection)
Invalid signatures return HTTP 400 with no internal error details in the response body
All five test cases pass, including the replay attack and tampered payload scenarios
The webhook handler correctly processes invoice.paid and payment_intent.succeeded events after successful validation

title: Pin Signal-CLI image to specific version and SHA256 digest, enable PVC encryption
description: The Signal-CLI deployment uses the mutable :latest tag, creating supply chain risk. The Signal-CLI PVC contains sensitive cryptographic material (Signal registration keys) with no encryption at rest and no backup strategy. This task pins the image, adds vulnerability scanning, enables PVC encryption, and implements backup.
subtasks: Identify the current stable Signal-CLI release version. Pin the image in the Signal-CLI Deployment manifest to a specific version tag AND SHA256 digest (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli:0.13.1@sha256:abc123...)

Add a CI step that runs Trivy against the Signal-CLI image before deployment: trivy image --severity CRITICAL,HIGH --exit-code 1 signalapp/signal-cli:<version>. Block deployment if CRITICAL vulnerabilities are found

Update the signal-cli-data PVC StorageClass to use an encrypted storage class (e.g., StorageClass with parameters.encrypted: true or a provider-specific encryption parameter). If the cluster storage provider does not support at-rest encryption natively, document the limitation and implement application-level encryption of the Signal-CLI data directory

Create a Kubernetes CronJob (signal-cli-backup) that runs daily, copies the Signal-CLI data directory to the R2 bucket sigma1-signal-backup with client-side AES-256 encryption before upload. Use openssl enc or age for encryption with a key stored in a Kubernetes Secret
Update the Morgan Deployment to use a StatefulSet instead of a Deployment to ensure PVC persistence across pod rescheduling, preventing Signal identity loss
Document the Signal-CLI data recovery procedure in ops/runbooks/signal-cli-recovery.md: download encrypted backup from R2, decrypt, restore to PVC, verify Signal registration
acceptance_criteria: Signal-CLI image is pinned to a specific version tag AND SHA256 digest in the manifest
CI pipeline includes Trivy scan of the Signal-CLI image blocking on CRITICAL/HIGH vulnerabilities
Signal-CLI PVC uses an encrypted StorageClass or has documented application-level encryption
Daily encrypted backups of Signal-CLI data are uploaded to R2 (verify backup CronJob runs successfully)
Morgan uses a StatefulSet with persistent PVC binding
ops/runbooks/signal-cli-recovery.md exists with tested recovery steps

title: Add SecurityContext and Pod Security Standards to all workloads
description: None of the Kubernetes Deployment manifests define SecurityContext settings. Without these, containers run as root by default and can potentially escape to the host. This task adds restrictive SecurityContext to every workload and enforces pod security standards at the namespace level.
subtasks: Add pod-level SecurityContext to every Deployment/StatefulSet manifest: runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, runAsGroup: 1000, fsGroup: 1000, seccompProfile: { type: RuntimeDefault }
Add container-level SecurityContext to every container in every Deployment/StatefulSet: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, capabilities: { drop: [ALL] }

For containers requiring writable directories (e.g., /tmp, application cache dirs), add emptyDir volume mounts with medium: Memory for tmpfs. Identify all services that write to the filesystem and add appropriate tmpfs mounts

Test the Signal-CLI container specifically — it writes to /home/signal-cli/.local/share/signal-cli which is on a PVC. Ensure readOnlyRootFilesystem: true works with the PVC mount and add any additional tmpfs mounts needed for signal-cli's temporary files

Apply the PodSecurity admission controller label to the sigma1, databases, openclaw, and signal namespaces: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted, pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted, pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
Verify every pod starts successfully with the restricted security context by running: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -l 'app.kubernetes.io/part-of=sigma1' and confirming all are Running/Ready
Test that no container can write to the root filesystem: kubectl exec <pod> -- touch /test-file should return 'Read-only file system' error
acceptance_criteria: Every Deployment and StatefulSet manifest includes pod-level SecurityContext with runAsNonRoot: true and seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault
Every container spec includes allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and capabilities drop ALL
All namespaces (sigma1, databases, openclaw, signal) have the restricted PodSecurity enforcement label
All pods start successfully and reach Ready state with the restricted security context
kubectl exec into any pod and running touch /test-file returns a read-only filesystem error
Services requiring writable directories use explicit tmpfs emptyDir mounts, not a writable root filesystem

title: Encrypt financial data at rest and apply column-level encryption for sensitive fields
description: The Finance service stores sensitive financial data including Stripe IDs, invoice amounts, and worker names in plaintext in PostgreSQL. There is no column-level encryption for high-value fields. This task implements column-level encryption using pgcrypto for the most sensitive fields and ensures the underlying storage uses encrypted volumes.
subtasks: Verify the CNPG PostgreSQL cluster's underlying StorageClass supports encryption at rest. If it does, document the encryption configuration. If not, enable it by updating the StorageClass parameters or switching to an encrypted StorageClass

Create a database migration adding pgcrypto-based column-level encryption for the following finance schema columns: invoices.stripe_invoice_id, payments.stripe_payment_id, and payroll_entries.worker_name. Use pgp_sym_encrypt with a key stored in a Kubernetes Secret sigma1-column-encryption-key

Update the Finance service's sqlx queries to use pgp_sym_decrypt when reading encrypted columns and pgp_sym_encrypt when writing. Ensure the encryption key is passed as a query parameter (not hardcoded in SQL)

Create a data migration script that encrypts existing plaintext data in the three target columns, converting from plaintext to pgcrypto-encrypted values without downtime (use a rolling migration pattern: add encrypted column → backfill → swap → drop old)
Add the sigma1-column-encryption-key Secret to the Finance service Deployment's projected volume mounts. The encryption key must not be exposed as an environment variable
Document the column encryption key management strategy in ops/runbooks/column-encryption.md including key rotation procedure (decrypt with old key, re-encrypt with new key)

Write integration tests verifying: (1) encrypted columns are not readable as plaintext via direct SQL query, (2) the Finance service correctly decrypts values in API responses, (3) new records are stored encrypted
acceptance_criteria: stripe_invoice_id, stripe_payment_id, and worker_name columns are encrypted at rest using pgcrypto
Direct SQL SELECT on encrypted columns returns ciphertext, not plaintext values
The Finance service API correctly decrypts and returns readable values in responses
Column encryption key is stored in a Kubernetes Secret and mounted as a file, not an environment variable
ops/runbooks/column-encryption.md documents the encryption key rotation procedure
Underlying PostgreSQL storage uses encrypted volumes (or limitation is documented with mitigation)

title: Harden SQL query building and add input sanitization for search parameters
description: The equipment catalog and finance services use dynamic query building with sqlx QueryBuilder for search and filtering. While sqlx parameterized queries prevent classic SQL injection, the dynamic ILIKE pattern construction can introduce injection risks if SQL wildcards are not escaped. This task ensures all dynamic query parameters use bind parameters and search inputs are properly sanitized.
subtasks: Audit all sqlx QueryBuilder usage in the equipment-catalog service (src/handlers/catalog.rs). Verify every dynamic WHERE clause value uses bind parameters ($1, $2, etc.) and never string interpolation or format!() macros

Implement a search input sanitizer function that escapes SQL ILIKE wildcards (%, _) in search strings before wrapping them in % for ILIKE matching: fn sanitize_search(input: &str) -> String { input.replace('%', '\\%').replace('_', '\\_') } then format!('%{}%', sanitized)
Apply the same audit and sanitizer to all dynamic query building in the finance service report handlers (src/handlers/reports.rs)

Add fuzz testing for the equipment-catalog search parameter using cargo-fuzz or proptest: generate random strings including SQL metacharacters (', ", %, _, ;, --, /*) and verify the query either succeeds without injection or returns a valid error
Add fuzz testing for any finance service dynamic query parameters with the same approach
Write unit tests for the sanitize_search function with edge cases: empty string, string of only %, string with Unicode, string exceeding 1000 characters
acceptance_criteria: All dynamic query parameters in equipment-catalog and finance services use sqlx bind parameters exclusively
Search inputs passed to ILIKE patterns have SQL wildcards (%, _) escaped before use
Fuzz testing with 10,000+ random inputs including SQL metacharacters produces no SQL injection or unexpected errors
The sanitize_search function has unit tests covering edge cases
No sqlx QueryBuilder usage in either service uses string interpolation for user-provided values

title: Require authentication on equipment API endpoint and restrict CORS on web chat
description: The equipment API machine-readable catalog endpoint (GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog) has no authentication, exposing the entire product catalog with pricing to unauthenticated requests. The Morgan web chat adapter configures CORS with wildcard origin (*), enabling any website to interact with Morgan. This task adds authentication to the equipment API and restricts CORS origins.
subtasks: Add JWT authentication requirement to GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog and POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout endpoints in the equipment-catalog service. These endpoints should require the same JWT validation as other /api/v1/* routes

If public access for AI agents is needed, implement an API key authentication mechanism: accept an X-API-Key header, validate against a list of issued API keys stored in a Kubernetes Secret sigma1-api-keys, and rate limit API key usage to 60 requests per minute per key

Update the Morgan web chat adapter configuration to restrict CORS origins from ['*'] to specific Sigma-1 domains: ['https://sigma1.com', 'https://www.sigma1.com', 'https://chat.sigma1.com']. Load allowed origins from an environment variable CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS for environment-specific configuration

Add the CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS environment variable to the Morgan Deployment manifest, with the production value set in the values file and a permissive value available only for the development environment

Write integration tests: (1) GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog without auth returns 401, (2) GET with valid JWT returns 200, (3) GET with valid API key returns 200, (4) CORS preflight from https://sigma1.com returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://sigma1.com, (5) CORS preflight from https://evil.com does not return Access-Control-Allow-Origin header
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog requires either a valid JWT or a valid API key
Unauthenticated requests to equipment-api endpoints return HTTP 401
API key authentication is rate limited to 60 requests per minute per key
Morgan web chat CORS is restricted to specific Sigma-1 domains (no wildcard *)
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS is configurable per environment via the Deployment manifest
CORS preflight requests from non-allowed origins do not receive Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers

title: Pin container base images to SHA256 digests and add Trivy scanning to CI/CD
description: Service Dockerfiles use base images like rust:1.75-slim and debian:bookworm-slim without SHA256 digest pinning. The CI/CD pipeline includes dependency scanning but not container image scanning. This task pins all base images and adds Trivy container scanning to the pipeline.
subtasks: For each service Dockerfile (equipment-catalog, rms, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine), identify the current base images and resolve their SHA256 digests using: docker pull <image>:<tag> && docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' <image>:<tag>
Update all builder stage FROM lines to include the SHA256 digest: e.g., FROM rust:1.75-slim@sha256:<digest> as builder
Update all runtime stage FROM lines to include the SHA256 digest: e.g., FROM debian:bookworm-slim@sha256:<digest>

Add a container image scanning step to the GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline (quality.yml or a new image-scan.yml): after docker build, run trivy image --severity CRITICAL,HIGH --exit-code 1 ghcr.io/sigma1/<service>:$TAG for each service
Configure the CI pipeline to block merge/deployment if Trivy reports any CRITICAL severity vulnerabilities (HIGH generates a warning but does not block)

Create a scheduled weekly GitHub Actions workflow (image-digest-check.yml) that checks if any pinned base image digests have newer patch versions available and creates a GitHub issue when updates are detected
acceptance_criteria: All Dockerfiles use base images pinned to SHA256 digests for both builder and runtime stages
CI/CD pipeline includes Trivy container image scanning for all five service images
Merge is blocked when Trivy detects CRITICAL vulnerabilities in any service image
A weekly scheduled workflow checks for base image updates and creates GitHub issues when detected
docker build for each service succeeds with the pinned digests

title: Create dedicated social media token Secret and implement audit logging middleware
description: Social media platform tokens are not explicitly managed in dedicated Kubernetes Secrets with proper scoping. Additionally, there is no general audit logging for administrative actions across services (product CRUD, invoice creation, social post approval). This task creates properly scoped social media secrets and implements a shared audit logging middleware.
subtasks: Create a dedicated Kubernetes Secret sigma1-social-tokens with explicit keys: INSTAGRAM_ACCESS_TOKEN, INSTAGRAM_BUSINESS_ACCOUNT_ID, LINKEDIN_ACCESS_TOKEN, LINKEDIN_ORG_ID, FACEBOOK_PAGE_ACCESS_TOKEN, FACEBOOK_PAGE_ID, TIKTOK_ACCESS_TOKEN. Document the required OAuth scopes for each platform in a comment or annotation
Update the social-engine Deployment to mount sigma1-social-tokens as a projected Secret volume rather than using envFrom. Remove any social tokens from other Secrets they may be co-located with

Implement token refresh logic in the social-engine service for platforms that support it (Instagram/Facebook Graph API long-lived tokens). On 401 responses from platform APIs, attempt automatic token refresh before failing
Add sigma1-social-tokens to the secret rotation CronJob reminder list and document platform-specific refresh procedures in ops/runbooks/secret-rotation.md

Implement a shared audit logging middleware pattern for the equipment-catalog service: intercept all POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE requests and INSERT into audit.admin_actions table with: timestamp, user_id (from JWT sub claim), http_method, resource_type (derived from route), resource_id (from path params), request_body_summary (first 500 chars, sensitive fields redacted)
Apply the same audit logging middleware to the RMS service for opportunity and project write operations
Apply the same audit logging middleware to the finance service for invoice and payroll write operations
Apply the same audit logging middleware to the social-engine service for draft approval and publish operations

Create the audit.admin_actions table via a database migration: id UUID PK, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, user_id TEXT, http_method TEXT, resource_type TEXT, resource_id TEXT, request_summary TEXT, source_service TEXT

Write integration tests: (1) POST to create a product generates an audit.admin_actions row, (2) the audit row contains correct user_id from JWT, (3) social media tokens are not accessible from non-social-engine pods
acceptance_criteria: Social media tokens are stored in a dedicated sigma1-social-tokens Secret with per-platform keys
Social tokens are mounted as projected volumes, not environment variables, in the social-engine Deployment only
Token refresh is attempted automatically on 401 responses from social platforms
All write operations (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) across all four services generate audit.admin_actions records
Audit records include user_id, http_method, resource_type, resource_id, and timestamp
Sensitive fields (passwords, tokens) are redacted from request_summary in audit records
ops/runbooks/secret-rotation.md includes platform-specific social token refresh procedures

title: Encrypt vetting service third-party PII in JSONB columns and add data retention
description: The customer vetting service stores third-party data (OpenCorporates company data, LinkedIn presence data, Google Reviews data) in JSONB columns without encryption or data retention policies. This data may be subject to the platforms' Terms of Service regarding storage. This task encrypts sensitive JSONB columns and implements automatic data purging.
subtasks: Apply pgcrypto encryption to the vetting_results.opencorporates_data JSONB column using pgp_sym_encrypt, similar to the approach used for finance data. Store the encryption key in a Kubernetes Secret sigma1-vetting-encryption-key
Update the customer-vetting service's sqlx queries to encrypt opencorporates_data on write and decrypt on read
Create a data migration to encrypt existing plaintext opencorporates_data values

Implement a scheduled tokio background task (or Kubernetes CronJob) that runs daily and deletes or anonymizes vetting_results rows where vetted_at is older than 90 days. Log each purge operation to the audit schema
Add a configuration option (VETTING_RETENTION_DAYS environment variable, default 90) to control the retention period
Review and document OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google Places API Terms of Service requirements for data storage and retention in ops/compliance/third-party-data-policy.md
Write integration tests: (1) opencorporates_data column contains ciphertext in raw SQL query, (2) API returns decrypted data correctly, (3) records older than 90 days are purged by the retention task
acceptance_criteria: vetting_results.opencorporates_data is encrypted at rest using pgcrypto
The vetting service correctly encrypts on write and decrypts on read
A daily retention task purges or anonymizes vetting records older than 90 days
VETTING_RETENTION_DAYS is configurable via environment variable
ops/compliance/third-party-data-policy.md documents ToS requirements for all three third-party data sources
Purge operations are logged to the audit schema
